---
title: "Ch 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes"
subtitle: "Cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere, hemisphere — flat nets that fold up"
lesson-id: "cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 11
ncert-chapter-title: "Surface Areas and Volumes"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.area-perimeter-volume", "cbse8.mensuration"]
unlocks: ["cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you compute the area of a rectangle and a circle?
   *(If no, revisit
   [t1.area-perimeter-volume](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you compute the volume of a cuboid? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse8.mensuration](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/09-mensuration/index.qmd).)*
3. Do you know that the area of a circle is $\pi r^2$ and circumference
   is $2 \pi r$?
4. Can you simplify $\pi \approx \tfrac{22}{7}$ when needed?

Three or four yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a cereal-box flap. Open it flat — you see a rectangular *net*.
Close it again — it folds into a cuboid. The **area** of the net is the
**total surface area** of the cuboid. The **volume** is what fills it
when you pour rice in.

Now imagine unrolling a tin can. The curved side flattens into a
rectangle whose width equals the can's height and whose length equals
the can's circumference $2 \pi r$. The two circular caps are obvious.

```{ojs}
viewof shapeChoice = Inputs.select(["cuboid", "cylinder", "cone", "sphere"], {value: "cylinder", label: "shape"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof rOrL = Inputs.range([1, 10], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "r or l (cm)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof hOrW = Inputs.range([1, 10], {value: 6, step: 1, label: "h or w (cm)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const PI = Math.PI;
  let lines = [];
  if (shapeChoice === "cuboid") {
    const l = rOrL, b = hOrW, h = 5;
    const v = l*b*h;
    const tsa = 2*(l*b + b*h + l*h);
    lines.push(`Cuboid l=${l}, b=${b}, h=${h}`);
    lines.push(`Volume = lbh = ${v} cm³`);
    lines.push(`TSA = 2(lb + bh + lh) = ${tsa} cm²`);
  } else if (shapeChoice === "cylinder") {
    const r = rOrL, h = hOrW;
    const v = PI*r*r*h;
    const tsa = 2*PI*r*(r + h);
    lines.push(`Cylinder r=${r}, h=${h}`);
    lines.push(`Volume = πr²h ≈ ${v.toFixed(2)} cm³`);
    lines.push(`TSA = 2πr(r + h) ≈ ${tsa.toFixed(2)} cm²`);
  } else if (shapeChoice === "cone") {
    const r = rOrL, h = hOrW;
    const l = Math.sqrt(r*r + h*h);
    const v = (1/3)*PI*r*r*h;
    const tsa = PI*r*(r + l);
    lines.push(`Cone r=${r}, h=${h}, slant l=${l.toFixed(2)}`);
    lines.push(`Volume = (1/3)πr²h ≈ ${v.toFixed(2)} cm³`);
    lines.push(`TSA = πr(r + l) ≈ ${tsa.toFixed(2)} cm²`);
  } else {
    const r = rOrL;
    const v = (4/3)*PI*r*r*r;
    const sa = 4*PI*r*r;
    lines.push(`Sphere r=${r}`);
    lines.push(`Volume = (4/3)πr³ ≈ ${v.toFixed(2)} cm³`);
    lines.push(`Surface = 4πr² ≈ ${sa.toFixed(2)} cm²`);
  }
  return html`<div style="font-family: monospace; font-size: 1rem; padding: 0.4rem 0;">
    ${lines.map(l => html`<div>${l}</div>`)}
  </div>`;
}
```

Pick a shape and adjust $r$ and $h$. Surface area = the net's area.
Volume = how much it holds. Watch how each formula scales.

## Wonder

1. Two cuboids of equal volume can have very different surface areas.
   Could you describe a "tall thin" one and a "short fat" one with the
   same volume?
2. Why is the volume of a cone exactly *one third* the volume of a
   cylinder with the same base and height?
3. The surface area of a sphere is $4 \pi r^2$ — exactly four times the
   area of its great circle. Is that an accident, or does it tell you
   something?
4. If you double the radius of a sphere, by what factor does its volume
   change?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q4 is a scaling question — volume scales as the *cube* of the radius.
Q2 we won't prove rigorously yet, but you can see it: pour cone-fulls
of water into a cylinder of the same base and height; you'll need
exactly three.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 (Cuboid)

A box has length $10$ cm, breadth $8$ cm, height $6$ cm. Compute its
volume and total surface area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$V = lbh$. $\text{TSA} = 2(lb + bh + lh)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = 10 \cdot 8 \cdot 6 = 480$ cm³.
$\text{TSA} = 2(80 + 48 + 60) = 2 \cdot 188 = 376$ cm².
</details>

### Problem 2 (Cylinder)

A cylindrical drum has radius $7$ cm and height $20$ cm. Find the
curved surface area, total surface area, and volume. Use $\pi =
\tfrac{22}{7}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
CSA $= 2 \pi r h$. TSA $= 2\pi r(r + h)$. $V = \pi r^2 h$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
CSA $= 2 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 20 = 880$ cm².
TSA $= 2 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 27 = 1188$ cm².
$V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49 \cdot 20 = 3080$ cm³.
</details>

### Problem 3 (Cone)

A cone has radius $r = 6$ cm and height $h = 8$ cm. Find slant height
$l$, then volume and total surface area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$. $V = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$. TSA $= \pi r(r +
l)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$l = \sqrt{36 + 64} = 10$ cm. $V = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi (36)(8) = 96 \pi
\approx 301.6$ cm³. TSA $= \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 16 = 96 \pi \approx
301.6$ cm².
</details>

### Problem 4 (Sphere)

A spherical ball has radius $5$ cm. Find its surface area and volume.
Use $\pi \approx 3.14$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
SA $= 4 \pi r^2$. $V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
SA $= 4 \pi \cdot 25 = 100 \pi \approx 314$ cm².
$V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi \cdot 125 = \tfrac{500 \pi}{3} \approx 523.3$ cm³.
</details>

### Problem 5 (Hemisphere)

A solid hemisphere has radius $7$ cm. Find its **total** surface area
(the curved hemisphere plus the flat circular base) and volume.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Curved surface of hemisphere $= 2 \pi r^2$. Add the flat circle
$\pi r^2$. Volume $= \tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
TSA $= 2 \pi r^2 + \pi r^2 = 3 \pi r^2 = 3 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49
= 462$ cm². Volume $= \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 343 =
\tfrac{2156 \cdot 343}{21}$… let's simplify: $\tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \pi
\cdot 343 = \tfrac{686}{3} \pi \approx 718.4$ cm³.
</details>

### Problem 6 (Conversion / pouring)

A cylinder of radius $6$ cm and height $14$ cm is full of water. The
water is poured into a hemispherical bowl. What must the bowl's radius
be so that the water exactly fills it?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volumes equal: $\pi r^2 h = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R^3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi (36)(14) = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R^3 \Rightarrow R^3 = \tfrac{3 \cdot
36 \cdot 14}{2} = 756 \Rightarrow R = \sqrt[3]{756} \approx 9.1$ cm.
</details>

You discovered: each shape has a flat-net way of seeing its surface area
and a multiplicative way of seeing its volume; conversion problems
(pouring water from one shape to another) are just volume-equation
problems.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

| Shape | Volume | Curved SA | Total SA |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Cuboid (l, b, h) | $lbh$ | — | $2(lb + bh + lh)$ |
| Cube (a) | $a^3$ | — | $6a^2$ |
| Cylinder (r, h) | $\pi r^2 h$ | $2 \pi r h$ | $2 \pi r (r + h)$ |
| Cone (r, h, slant l) | $\tfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$ | $\pi r l$ | $\pi r (r + l)$ |
| Sphere (r) | $\tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | $4 \pi r^2$ | $4 \pi r^2$ |
| Hemisphere (r) | $\tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3$ | $2 \pi r^2$ | $3 \pi r^2$ |

For the cone, $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$ (Pythagorean slant height).

When converting between shapes (melting, pouring, recasting), the
**volume is conserved**, not the surface area.

:::

A short verse:

> *Cuboid: l-b-h, sum the rectangles by twos.*
> *Cylinder: a wrapped rectangle and two circles.*
> *Cone: a third of cylinder; slant is hypotenuse.*
> *Sphere: four pi r squared, four-thirds pi r cubed.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A solid metal cylinder of radius $6$ cm and height $32$ cm is melted
> down and recast into spheres of radius $4$ cm. How many spheres can
> be made?

A competent solver:

1. **Volume of cylinder** = $\pi (6)^2 (32) = 1152 \pi$ cm³.
2. **Volume of one sphere** = $\tfrac{4}{3} \pi (4)^3 = \tfrac{256 \pi}{3}$
   cm³.
3. **Number of spheres** = $\dfrac{1152 \pi}{256 \pi / 3} = \dfrac{1152 \cdot 3}{256}
   = \dfrac{3456}{256} = 13.5$.
4. Since you can't make half a sphere from melted metal (well, you can,
   but it's not a sphere), the most full spheres = **13**.

**Answer:** $13$ complete spheres, with metal left over.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A cube has edge $5$ cm. Find its volume and surface area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = 125$ cm³. SA $= 150$ cm².
</details>

**W2.** A cylinder has radius $14$ cm and height $10$ cm. Find its
volume. Use $\pi = \tfrac{22}{7}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 196 \cdot 10 = 6160$ cm³.
</details>

**W3.** A sphere has surface area $616$ cm². Find its radius. Use
$\pi = \tfrac{22}{7}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$4 \pi r^2 = 616$. Solve.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$r^2 = \tfrac{616}{4 \pi} = \tfrac{616 \cdot 7}{4 \cdot 22} =
\tfrac{4312}{88} = 49$. So $r = 7$ cm.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A conical tent has base radius $7$ m and slant height $25$ m.
How much canvas is needed (curved surface only)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
CSA of cone = $\pi r l$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi r l = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 25 = 550$ m².
</details>

**P2.** A rectangular box has length $20$ cm, breadth $15$ cm, and
height $10$ cm. How many small cubes of edge $5$ cm fit inside?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume divided by volume — but check that the cubes tile.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{20 \cdot 15 \cdot 10}{125} = \tfrac{3000}{125} = 24$. And
$20, 15, 10$ are all divisible by $5$, so they tile cleanly. **24
cubes.**
</details>

**P3.** A hemispherical bowl has inner radius $14$ cm. How much water
in litres can it hold (1 litre $= 1000$ cm³)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3 = \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 14^3
= \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 2744$. Simplify:
$\tfrac{2 \cdot 22 \cdot 2744}{21} = \tfrac{120736}{21} \approx
5749.3$ cm³ $\approx$ **5.75 litres**.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A right circular cylinder is just enclosing a sphere of
radius $r$ (i.e. the sphere's diameter is the cylinder's height *and*
the cylinder's diameter). Show that the surface area of the sphere
equals the curved surface area of the cylinder.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cylinder height $= 2r$. CSA $= 2 \pi r (2r)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
CSA of cylinder $= 2 \pi r \cdot 2r = 4 \pi r^2$. SA of sphere $=
4 \pi r^2$. **Equal.** This is a famous identity discovered by
Archimedes — he was so proud of it that he asked for it to be carved
on his tomb.
</details>

**S2.** A toy is made of a hemisphere on top and a cone underneath,
each with radius $7$ cm. The total height of the toy is $31$ cm. Find
its total surface area (the part you can paint).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cone height $= 31 - 7 = 24$ cm. Slant height $= \sqrt{7^2 + 24^2} = 25$.
The painted surface is the cone's curved surface plus the hemisphere's
curved surface (the flat circle is hidden where they join).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{SA} = \pi r l + 2 \pi r^2 = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 \cdot 25 +
2 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49 = 550 + 308 = 858$ cm².
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The total surface area of a cube of edge $a$
is:
(a) $a^3$ (b) $a^2$ (c) $4a^2$ (d) $6a^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(d) $6a^2$.**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the volume of a cylinder whose base
radius is $3.5$ cm and height $10$ cm. Use $\pi = \tfrac{22}{7}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \tfrac{22}{7} \cdot 12.25 \cdot 10 = \tfrac{2695}{7} = 385$ cm³.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** A solid cylinder of radius $4$ cm and
height $30$ cm is melted and recast into a single solid cone of base
radius $5$ cm. Find the height of the cone.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volumes equal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi (16)(30) = \tfrac{1}{3} \pi (25) h \Rightarrow 480 = \tfrac{25
h}{3} \Rightarrow h = \tfrac{1440}{25} = 57.6$ cm.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** A cylindrical pipe has external diameter $10$
cm, internal diameter $8$ cm, and length $7$ m. The pipe is made of
metal weighing $9.5$ g/cm³. Find the mass of the pipe in kilograms.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume of the metal shell = (outer volume) − (inner volume). Convert
length to cm.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$V = \pi (R^2 - r^2) h$ with $R = 5, r = 4, h = 700$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \pi (25 - 16) (700) = 6300 \pi$ cm³ $= 6300 \cdot \tfrac{22}{7}
= 19800$ cm³.
Mass $= 19800 \cdot 9.5 = 188100$ g $= 188.1$ kg.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 11, Problem 11.5
(melting and recasting, similar to 3-mark). **Previous-year pointer:**
CBSE Class 9 sample paper "find mass of cylindrical pipe" (similar to
5-mark).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Surface area of a sphere is $\pi r^2$."** That's the area of a
   *flat circle* of the same radius. Sphere SA is **four times** that:
   $4 \pi r^2$.
2. **"Slant height of a cone is the same as its height."** Almost
   never. Slant $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$; only when $r = 0$ (a flat
   needle) do they coincide.
3. **"You can sum surface areas when joining two shapes."** No — you
   *subtract* the area where the two meet, because that face is hidden
   inside the joined object.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state every formula in the *Name* table without notes?
2. Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
3. Could you do a fresh "melt and recast" problem and conserve volume
   correctly?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Statistics**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* 6 (volume conservation) and the
  *Name* table — these need rewriting from memory.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)
  — area and volume meaning.
- [`cbse8.mensuration`](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/09-mensuration/index.qmd)
  — formulas for cuboid and cylinder; surface and volume.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/13-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)
  — combinations of solids, frustum of a cone.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 11.1–11.4: cylinders, cones,
  spheres, hemispheres.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different angle):
  [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)
  presents the cuboid and cylinder; the cone-and-sphere additions are
  this chapter's contribution.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Bounding spheres and bounding cylinders are the
fastest-to-test collision shapes. The volume formulas you just learned
control physics simulation costs in every modern engine.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Archimedes' identity from S1 — sphere SA $=$ cylinder CSA
when one inscribes the other — was rediscovered by integral calculus
(volume of revolution). It is the cleanest "Aha!" of classical solid
geometry, predating calculus by 1900 years.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (cereal-box net Touch),
  Archimedes (S1), Bhāskara-style table for the *Name* beat.
:::
